projectCreation.heroTitle: Learn Machine Learning by Actually Doing It
projectCreation.heroSubtitle: Not videos. Not slides. You build, train, and test real ML models — step by step, with guidance at every turn. Understand the why, not just the how.

handsOn.title: Hands-On Learning

noCode.title: No Code Required
noCode.description: Designed for students and beginners. Just describe what you want to build and follow the steps

pricing.title: Simple, transparent pricing

free.name: Free
free.description: Learn ML concepts with interactive simulations — no signup required

pro.name: Pro
pro.description: Real model training in your browser — train, test, and predict

school.name: School
school.description: Per-student pricing for classrooms. Minimum 6 students.

items.question: Can I upgrade or downgrade anytime?
items.answer: Yes, you can change your plan at any time. Upgrades take effect immediately, downgrades at the end of your billing period.

items.question: What happens when I reach my free tier limits?
items.answer: You'll see an upgrade prompt. Your existing data is never deleted — you just can't create new resources until you upgrade or the month resets.

items.question: Is there a free trial for Pro?
items.answer: Yes! Pro comes with a 14-day free trial. No credit card required.

items.question: Do you offer discounts for non-profit organizations or schools?
items.answer: Yes, contact us at non-profit@modelmentor.ai for educational discounts.

pricing.checkoutSuccess: Payment successful! Your plan has been upgraded.

iste-5c.name: Computational Thinker - Break Problems

iste-3d.name: Knowledge Constructor - Build Knowledge

pensamientoComputacional.description: Aligned with SEP Pensamiento Computacional area — students use algorithmic thinking and data analysis to solve problems

image-classification-6-8.title: Introduction to Image Classification
image-classification-6-8.overview: Students explore how computers can learn to recognize and classify images by training their own image classification model. Using everyday objects like flowers and animals, students collect image data, train a model using ModelMentor, and evaluate its accuracy. This introductory lesson builds foundational understanding of how machine learning works through hands-on experimentation.
objectives: Explain how image classification models learn to recognize patterns in visual data
Collect and organize a labeled image dataset with at least two categories
Train an image classification model and interpret its accuracy metrics
materials: Computers or tablets with internet access and ModelMentor loaded
Sample image sets of flowers and animals (provided in ModelMentor)
Whiteboard or projector for class demonstrations

vocabulary.definition: Teaching a computer to sort images into categories, like telling apart photos of cats and dogs
The collection of labeled example images that the computer uses to learn patterns
A tag that tells the computer what category an image belongs to (e.g., flower or animal)
How often the model correctly identifies the category of an image, shown as a percentage

steps: Show students a set of 6 mixed images (3 flowers, 3 animals) and ask them to sort them into groups
Ask: How did you decide which group each image belongs to? What features did you look for?
teacherNotes: Use this activity to draw parallels between human pattern recognition and machine learning. Students naturally identify features like petals, fur, legs, etc.
discussionPrompts: What clues did you use to tell flowers apart from animals? Could you teach someone else your method?

steps: Introduce the concept of image classification: computers learning to sort images into categories
Explain the training process: showing the computer many labeled examples so it can find patterns
Demonstrate opening ModelMentor and navigating to the image classification project type
teacherNotes: Emphasize that the computer does not understand images the way humans do — it looks at pixel patterns and colors
discussionPrompts: How is the way a computer looks at images different from how you look at them?
Why do you think we need to show the computer many examples instead of just one?

steps: Guide students through loading the sample flower/animal dataset in ModelMentor
Walk through the training process together, explaining each step as it happens
Have students observe the accuracy metric as the model trains and discuss what it means
discussionPrompts: What do you notice happening to the accuracy number as the model trains? Why do you think it changes?

steps: Students test the trained model with new images not in the training set
Students record which predictions are correct and which are wrong in their handout
discussionPrompts: Did the model get any predictions wrong? What do you think might have confused it?

steps: Class discussion: What surprised you about how the model performed?
Exit ticket: Write one thing the model did well and one thing it struggled with
discussionPrompts: If you could give the model more training data, what kind of images would you add to help it improve?

indicators: Students can explain in their own words how the model learns from examples
Students correctly identify whether model predictions are accurate or inaccurate
Students suggest reasonable improvements to the training data to improve accuracy

conceptualUnderstanding.beginning: Cannot explain how the model learns from images; confuses classification with other tasks
conceptualUnderstanding.developing: Provides a basic explanation of classification but misses key concepts like training data or labels
conceptualUnderstanding.proficient: Clearly explains how the model uses labeled training data to learn to classify new images
conceptualUnderstanding.advanced: Explains the classification process and identifies factors that affect model performance such as data quality and quantity

dataCollection.beginning: Unable to collect or organize images into labeled categories without significant assistance
dataCollection.developing: Collects images but labels are inconsistent or categories are poorly defined
dataCollection.proficient: Collects a well-organized dataset with clear, consistent labels for each category
dataCollection.advanced: Creates a balanced, diverse dataset and explains why variety in training data matters for model performance

modelTraining.beginning: Cannot initiate training or interpret the accuracy metric
modelTraining.developing: Can start training but struggles to explain what accuracy means or why it changes
modelTraining.proficient: Successfully trains a model and correctly interprets accuracy as a measure of correct predictions
modelTraining.advanced: Trains the model, interprets accuracy, and proposes specific strategies to improve model performance

criticalThinking.beginning: Cannot identify model errors or suggest reasons for incorrect predictions
criticalThinking.developing: Identifies some errors but cannot explain why the model might have made mistakes
criticalThinking.proficient: Identifies errors and provides reasonable explanations for why the model struggled with certain images
criticalThinking.advanced: Analyzes error patterns, connects them to training data limitations, and proposes data-driven solutions

strategies: Provide a pre-labeled dataset so students can focus on training rather than data collection
Use the ModelMentor guided tour mode which provides step-by-step prompts
Pair with a peer mentor who can help explain concepts in student-friendly language
Follow the standard lesson flow with the provided flower/animal dataset
Encourage students to test with their own images found online
Challenge students to add a third category to their classification model
Ask students to deliberately create a biased dataset and observe how it affects accuracy
Have students write a brief explanation of how they would improve the model for a younger student

handout.title: Image Classification Explorer - Student Handout
handout.instructions: Follow along with the class activity. Record your observations and predictions in the spaces below.

sections.heading: Part 1: Exploring the Data
prompts: How many images are in your training dataset? How many categories do you have?
Describe what makes images in each category similar to each other.
reflectionQuestions: Why do you think the computer needs many examples of each category to learn?

sections.heading: Part 2: Training the Model
prompts: What was the model accuracy after training? Write the percentage here.
Did the accuracy go up or down during training? Describe what you observed.

sections.heading: Part 3: Testing and Reflection
prompts: Test your model with 5 new images. Record the results: Image description, Predicted category, Correct? (Yes/No)
reflectionQuestions: What types of images did the model get wrong? What might have confused it?
If you could add more training images, what would you add to help the model improve?

workflowSteps: Open ModelMentor and select Image Classification project
Load the sample dataset or upload your own images
Review your data in the preview panel
Click Train Model and observe the accuracy metric

misconceptions.misconception: The computer understands what is in the image like a human does
misconceptions.correction: The computer only detects patterns in pixel values (colors, shapes, edges). It has no understanding of what a flower or animal actually is.

misconceptions.misconception: More training data always means a better model
misconceptions.correction: Quality matters as much as quantity. Blurry, mislabeled, or unrepresentative images can actually hurt model performance.

misconceptions.misconception: If the model gets 100% accuracy on training data, it will work perfectly on new images
misconceptions.correction: High training accuracy can mean the model memorized the examples rather than learning general patterns. It may still fail on new, unseen images.

classroomTips: Have students work in pairs to encourage discussion about why the model makes certain predictions
If internet is slow, pre-download the sample dataset before class
Use the projector to show one student group training in real-time so the whole class can observe

warmUp: What clues did you use to tell flowers apart from animals? Could you teach someone else your method?
directInstruction: How is the way a computer looks at images different from how you look at them?
Why do you think we need to show the computer many examples instead of just one?
guidedPractice: What do you notice happening to the accuracy number as the model trains? Why do you think it changes?
closure: If you could give the model more training data, what kind of images would you add to help it improve?

image-classification-9-12.title: Advanced Image Classification with Transfer Learning
image-classification-9-12.overview: Students deepen their understanding of image classification by exploring transfer learning, confusion matrices, and model evaluation metrics. Using ModelMentor, students fine-tune a pre-trained model on a custom dataset, analyze per-class performance using a confusion matrix, and evaluate precision, recall, and F1 scores. This lesson connects to real-world applications like medical imaging and autonomous vehicles.
objectives: Explain how transfer learning leverages pre-trained models to improve classification on new tasks
Interpret a confusion matrix to identify which classes the model confuses most often
Calculate and explain precision, recall, and F1 score for a multi-class classifier
Evaluate trade-offs between model complexity, training time, and accuracy
materials: Computers with internet access and ModelMentor loaded
Whiteboard or projector for demonstrating confusion matrix interpretation

vocabulary.definition: A technique where a model trained on a large dataset is adapted to a new, smaller dataset — like using knowledge from one task to help with another
A table that shows how often the model correctly and incorrectly classifies each category, revealing which classes get confused with each other
Of all the items the model predicted as a certain class, what fraction actually belonged to that class
Of all the items that actually belong to a class, what fraction did the model correctly identify
A single number that balances precision and recall — useful when you want both to be high

steps: Show students two scenarios: training a model from scratch with 50 images vs. fine-tuning a pre-trained model with 50 images
Ask students to predict which approach will perform better and why
discussionPrompts: If you already knew how to identify dogs, would it be easier to learn to identify wolves? Why?

steps: Explain transfer learning: how pre-trained models already know basic visual features (edges, textures, shapes)
Introduce the confusion matrix as a tool for understanding model errors beyond simple accuracy
Walk through precision, recall, and F1 score calculations with a simple 2-class example
Discuss real-world applications where these metrics matter (medical imaging: recall is critical; spam filtering: precision is critical)
teacherNotes: Use the analogy of a student transferring schools — they already know math basics, they just need to learn the new curriculum specifics
discussionPrompts: In medical imaging, would you rather have high precision or high recall? What are the consequences of each type of error?
Why might overall accuracy be misleading when classes are imbalanced?

steps: Guide students through loading a multi-class dataset and enabling transfer learning in ModelMentor
Train the model together and examine the confusion matrix output
Calculate precision and recall for one class as a group exercise
discussionPrompts: Looking at the confusion matrix, which classes does the model confuse most? What visual similarities might explain this?

steps: Students train their own model with transfer learning enabled and analyze the confusion matrix
Students calculate precision, recall, and F1 for at least two classes
Students experiment with adding more training data for confused classes and retrain
discussionPrompts: After adding more data for the confused classes, did the confusion matrix improve? By how much?

steps: Groups present their confusion matrix findings and strategies for improvement
Class discussion: When would you choose transfer learning over training from scratch?
discussionPrompts: What real-world problem would you want to solve with image classification, and which metric (precision or recall) would matter most?

indicators: Students can explain why transfer learning is more efficient than training from scratch with limited data
Students correctly interpret confusion matrix cells and identify the most confused class pairs
Students calculate precision and recall and explain the trade-off between them

conceptualUnderstanding.beginning: Cannot explain what transfer learning is or why it might be useful
conceptualUnderstanding.developing: Provides a basic definition of transfer learning but cannot explain its advantages over training from scratch
conceptualUnderstanding.proficient: Clearly explains how transfer learning reuses learned features and why it works better with limited data
conceptualUnderstanding.advanced: Explains transfer learning, identifies when it is and is not appropriate, and connects to real-world applications

modelEvaluation.beginning: Cannot read or interpret a confusion matrix
modelEvaluation.developing: Can identify correct predictions on the diagonal but struggles to interpret off-diagonal errors
modelEvaluation.proficient: Correctly interprets the full confusion matrix and identifies which classes are most often confused
modelEvaluation.advanced: Uses the confusion matrix to diagnose specific model weaknesses and proposes targeted data collection strategies

transferLearning.beginning: Cannot enable or configure transfer learning in the tool
transferLearning.developing: Enables transfer learning but cannot explain the difference in results compared to training from scratch
transferLearning.proficient: Successfully applies transfer learning and compares results with and without it, noting improvements
transferLearning.advanced: Experiments with different pre-trained base models and analyzes how the choice affects performance on specific classes

criticalThinking.beginning: Cannot calculate or explain precision, recall, or F1 score
criticalThinking.developing: Can calculate one metric but confuses precision with recall or cannot explain their significance
criticalThinking.proficient: Correctly calculates precision, recall, and F1, and explains when each metric is most important
criticalThinking.advanced: Analyzes metric trade-offs in context, proposes threshold adjustments, and connects to real-world decision-making

strategies: Provide a pre-computed confusion matrix and guide interpretation with color-coded cells
Use the ModelMentor guided tour to walk through transfer learning setup step by step
Simplify to a 2-class problem before attempting multi-class classification
Follow the standard lesson with a 5-class dataset and full metric calculations
Encourage peer discussion about confusion matrix patterns
Challenge students to compare multiple pre-trained base models and analyze which works best for their data
Have students research and present a real-world case where precision vs. recall trade-offs had significant consequences
Ask students to create an imbalanced dataset intentionally and analyze how it affects metrics

handout.title: Advanced Image Classification - Student Handout
handout.instructions: Complete each section as you work through the lesson. Show your calculations and explain your reasoning.

sections.heading: Part 1: Transfer Learning Experiment
prompts: Train your model WITHOUT transfer learning. Record the accuracy: ____%
Train your model WITH transfer learning. Record the accuracy: ____%
reflectionQuestions: Why did transfer learning produce different results? What prior knowledge did the pre-trained model bring?

sections.heading: Part 2: Confusion Matrix Analysis
prompts: Draw or paste your confusion matrix below. Circle the two classes that are most often confused.
For your most confused class pair, count: True Positives = ___, False Positives = ___, False Negatives = ___

sections.heading: Part 3: Metrics and Improvement
prompts: Calculate Precision = TP/(TP+FP) = ___ and Recall = TP/(TP+FN) = ___ for your chosen class
reflectionQuestions: What specific images could you add to your training data to reduce confusion between the two most confused classes?
In what real-world application would high recall be more important than high precision? Explain why.

workflowSteps: Open ModelMentor and create a new Image Classification project
Train once without transfer learning, record results
Enable transfer learning and retrain, compare results
Analyze the confusion matrix and calculate metrics

misconceptions.misconception: Transfer learning always produces better results than training from scratch
misconceptions.correction: Transfer learning works best when the pre-trained model was trained on similar data. If the new task is very different (e.g., medical X-rays vs. natural photos), it may not help much.

misconceptions.misconception: A high overall accuracy means the model works well for all classes
misconceptions.correction: With imbalanced datasets, a model can achieve high accuracy by mostly predicting the majority class while performing poorly on minority classes.

misconceptions.misconception: The confusion matrix only shows errors
misconceptions.correction: The diagonal of the confusion matrix shows correct predictions. The off-diagonal cells show errors, revealing which specific classes get confused.

classroomTips: Have students color-code their confusion matrices (green for correct, red for errors) to make patterns more visible
Use real-world examples like medical diagnosis to make precision/recall trade-offs concrete and engaging
Allow students to choose their own image categories to increase engagement and ownership

warmUp: If you already knew how to identify dogs, would it be easier to learn to identify wolves? Why?
directInstruction: In medical imaging, would you rather have high precision or high recall? What are the consequences of each type of error?
Why might overall accuracy be misleading when classes are imbalanced?
guidedPractice: Looking at the confusion matrix, which classes does the model confuse most? What visual similarities might explain this?
closure: What real-world problem would you want to solve with image classification, and which metric would matter most?

text-classification-9-12.title: Text Classification: Sentiment Analysis and Spam Detection
text-classification-9-12.overview: Students explore natural language processing (NLP) by building text classification models for sentiment analysis and spam detection. They learn how computers process text through tokenization, build training datasets from real-world text samples, and evaluate model performance on unseen text. This lesson connects to everyday applications like email filtering, social media moderation, and customer feedback analysis.
objectives: Explain how text is converted into numerical representations that machine learning models can process
Design and curate a labeled text dataset for a binary classification task
Train a text classification model and evaluate its performance using accuracy and per-class metrics
Analyze how word choice, context, and ambiguity affect model predictions
materials: Computers with internet access and ModelMentor loaded
Sample text datasets: movie reviews (sentiment) and email samples (spam/not-spam)
Whiteboard or projector for demonstrating tokenization concepts

vocabulary.definition: The process of breaking text into smaller pieces (tokens) like words or subwords that the model can process individually
Using a model to determine the emotional tone of text — whether it expresses positive, negative, or neutral feelings
The field of AI focused on helping computers understand, interpret, and generate human language
A simple text representation that counts how often each word appears, ignoring word order and grammar

steps: Display 5 short text messages on screen and ask students to classify each as positive, negative, or neutral
Reveal that one message is sarcastic — discuss why it is hard to classify
Ask: What makes text classification harder than image classification for computers?
discussionPrompts: What clues do you use to determine if a message is positive or negative? Are those clues always reliable?

steps: Explain how computers cannot read text directly — they need numerical representations
Demonstrate tokenization: break a sentence into words, then show how each word maps to a number
Introduce the bag-of-words concept and its limitations (loses word order and context)
Show real-world examples: spam filters, sentiment analysis on product reviews, content moderation
teacherNotes: Use a physical demonstration: write a sentence on cards (one word per card), shuffle them, and ask if the meaning changes — this illustrates why word order matters
discussionPrompts: If we shuffle the words in a sentence, does the meaning always change? Can you think of examples where it does and does not?
Why might a spam filter incorrectly flag a legitimate email? What words might trigger it?

steps: Guide students through loading the movie review sentiment dataset in ModelMentor
Walk through the text preprocessing steps together: tokenization and vocabulary building
Train the sentiment model as a class and examine which words the model associates with positive vs. negative
discussionPrompts: Which words does the model think are most positive? Most negative? Do you agree with its choices?

steps: Students choose between sentiment analysis or spam detection for their independent project
Students curate their own small dataset (at least 20 samples per class) or use the provided samples
Students train, test, and analyze their model, documenting cases where it fails
discussionPrompts: Find a text sample that your model classifies incorrectly. Why do you think it failed? What about the text is ambiguous?

steps: Students share their most interesting misclassification and explain why the model was confused
Class discussion: What are the ethical implications of automated text classification in content moderation?
discussionPrompts: Should social media companies use AI to automatically remove content? What could go wrong?

indicators: Students can explain the tokenization process and why text must be converted to numbers
Students successfully curate a labeled dataset with clear category definitions
Students identify and explain specific cases where context or ambiguity causes misclassification

conceptualUnderstanding.beginning: Cannot explain how text is converted to a format the model can use
conceptualUnderstanding.developing: Knows text must be converted but cannot explain tokenization or numerical representation
conceptualUnderstanding.proficient: Clearly explains tokenization and how words are mapped to numerical representations for model input
conceptualUnderstanding.advanced: Explains multiple text representation methods, their trade-offs, and how context affects meaning

dataPreparation.beginning: Cannot create a labeled dataset or labels are inconsistent and unclear
dataPreparation.developing: Creates a dataset but with ambiguous labels, insufficient samples, or class imbalance
dataPreparation.proficient: Curates a well-labeled, balanced dataset with clear category definitions and sufficient samples
dataPreparation.advanced: Creates a high-quality dataset, identifies edge cases, and explains how data quality affects model performance

modelTraining.beginning: Cannot train the model or interpret any results
modelTraining.developing: Trains the model but cannot explain results beyond overall accuracy
modelTraining.proficient: Trains the model, interprets per-class performance, and identifies specific failure patterns
modelTraining.advanced: Analyzes model behavior in depth, identifies systematic biases, and proposes targeted improvements

criticalThinking.beginning: Cannot identify situations where text classification might fail
criticalThinking.developing: Identifies that sarcasm or ambiguity is hard but cannot explain why in technical terms
criticalThinking.proficient: Explains specific linguistic phenomena (sarcasm, context, ambiguity) that challenge text models
criticalThinking.advanced: Connects NLP limitations to real-world ethical concerns and proposes mitigation strategies

strategies: Provide a pre-curated dataset so students can focus on training and analysis rather than data collection
Use the ModelMentor guided tour for text classification setup
Start with a simple binary task (positive/negative) with clearly unambiguous examples
Follow the standard lesson with choice between sentiment analysis and spam detection
Encourage students to find and test edge cases that challenge their model
Challenge students to build a multi-class sentiment model (positive, negative, neutral, mixed)
Have students research and present on a real NLP failure case (biased language models, mistranslation incidents)
Ask students to compare bag-of-words performance with more advanced text representations

handout.title: Text Classification Lab - Student Handout
handout.instructions: Complete each section as you build your text classification model. Record observations and analysis.

sections.heading: Part 1: Understanding Tokenization
prompts: Take this sentence and break it into tokens (words): "I really loved this movie but the ending was disappointing"
How many tokens does your sentence have? Which tokens do you think signal positive sentiment? Negative?
reflectionQuestions: Why might the word "but" be important for understanding sentiment even though it is not positive or negative itself?

sections.heading: Part 2: Building Your Dataset
prompts: What classification task did you choose? (sentiment / spam / other: ___)
How many samples do you have in each class? Class A: ___ samples, Class B: ___ samples

sections.heading: Part 3: Model Analysis
prompts: Record your model accuracy: ___%. Find 2 misclassified examples and write them below with your explanation of why the model failed.
reflectionQuestions: What patterns does your model rely on most? Are there cases where those patterns are misleading?
How could automated text classification cause harm if used without human oversight?

workflowSteps: Open ModelMentor and select Text Classification project type
Load the sample dataset or create your own labeled text samples
Review the tokenization preview to understand how your text is processed
Train the model and observe accuracy metrics
Test with new text samples and analyze misclassifications

misconceptions.misconception: The model understands the meaning of words like humans do
misconceptions.correction: The model only learns statistical patterns — which words tend to appear together in each category. It has no understanding of meaning, context, or intent.

misconceptions.misconception: Longer text is always easier to classify correctly
misconceptions.correction: Longer text can contain mixed signals (both positive and negative statements), making classification harder. Short, clear statements can be easier to classify.

misconceptions.misconception: A spam filter only looks for specific banned words
misconceptions.correction: Modern spam filters use many features including word patterns, sender information, formatting, and statistical models — not just keyword matching.

classroomTips: Use examples from social media or messaging that students relate to — this increases engagement significantly
Have students try to trick each other models with adversarial examples — this builds critical thinking
Discuss content moderation ethics early to frame the technical work in a real-world context

warmUp: What clues do you use to determine if a message is positive or negative? Are those clues always reliable?
directInstruction: If we shuffle the words in a sentence, does the meaning always change? Can you think of examples where it does and does not?
Why might a spam filter incorrectly flag a legitimate email? What words might trigger it?
guidedPractice: Which words does the model think are most positive? Most negative? Do you agree with its choices?
closure: Should social media companies use AI to automatically remove content? What could go wrong?

regression-9-12.title: Regression Models: Predicting House Prices
regression-9-12.overview: Students learn how regression models predict continuous numerical values by building a house price prediction model. They explore linear relationships between features (square footage, bedrooms, location) and price, train a regression model using ModelMentor, and evaluate predictions using Mean Squared Error (MSE) and R-squared metrics. This lesson connects mathematics concepts like slope and correlation to real-world machine learning applications.
objectives: Explain how regression differs from classification and identify problems suited for each approach
Identify linear and non-linear relationships between input features and a target variable
Train a regression model and interpret Mean Squared Error (MSE) and R-squared as measures of prediction quality
Analyze how feature selection and data quality affect prediction accuracy
materials: Computers with internet access and ModelMentor loaded
House price dataset with features: square footage, bedrooms, bathrooms, year built, location score
Graph paper or digital graphing tool for visualizing relationships

vocabulary.definition: A type of machine learning that predicts a continuous number (like price or temperature) rather than a category
When two variables change together at a constant rate — as one goes up, the other goes up (or down) proportionally
A measure of how far off predictions are from actual values — calculated by averaging the squared differences between predicted and actual values
A mathematical formula that measures how wrong the model predictions are — the model tries to minimize this during training
A score from 0 to 1 that indicates how well the model explains the variation in the data — 1.0 means perfect predictions, 0 means no better than guessing the average

steps: Show students a scatter plot of house square footage vs. price and ask them to draw a line that best fits the data
Ask: If a house is 2000 sq ft, what would you predict its price to be based on your line?
Reveal the actual prices and discuss how close their predictions were
discussionPrompts: How did you decide where to draw your line? What made some lines better than others?

steps: Explain the difference between regression (predicting numbers) and classification (predicting categories)
Introduce the concept of a loss function: how the model measures its own errors during training
Walk through MSE calculation with a simple 3-point example on the board
Explain R-squared as a measure of how much of the variation in prices the model can explain
teacherNotes: Connect to prior math knowledge: slope of a line, y = mx + b, correlation. Students who have taken algebra will recognize these concepts.
discussionPrompts: Why do we square the errors in MSE instead of just averaging them? What would happen with negative errors?
If a model has R-squared of 0.85, what does that tell us? Is that good enough for predicting house prices?

steps: Guide students through loading the house price dataset in ModelMentor
Explore the data together: create scatter plots of each feature vs. price to identify relationships
Train the regression model as a class and interpret the MSE and R-squared output
discussionPrompts: Which feature has the strongest relationship with price? How can you tell from the scatter plot?

steps: Students experiment with different feature combinations to find the best predictor set
Students train multiple models and compare MSE and R-squared values
Students test their best model with custom house descriptions and evaluate if predictions seem reasonable
discussionPrompts: Which combination of features gave you the lowest MSE? Were you surprised by which features mattered most?

steps: Groups share their best feature combinations and model performance metrics
Class discussion: What are the limitations of using a model to predict house prices? What factors might it miss?
discussionPrompts: Would you trust this model to set the price for your house? What additional information would you want?

indicators: Students correctly distinguish between regression and classification problems with real-world examples
Students interpret MSE values and explain what a lower MSE means for prediction quality
Students identify which features have the strongest predictive relationship with the target variable

conceptualUnderstanding.beginning: Cannot explain what regression is or how it differs from classification
conceptualUnderstanding.developing: Knows regression predicts numbers but cannot explain loss functions or evaluation metrics
conceptualUnderstanding.proficient: Clearly explains regression, loss functions, and interprets MSE and R-squared correctly
conceptualUnderstanding.advanced: Explains regression concepts, identifies assumptions and limitations, and connects to mathematical foundations

dataAnalysis.beginning: Cannot identify relationships between features and the target variable
dataAnalysis.developing: Identifies obvious linear relationships but misses non-linear patterns or feature interactions
dataAnalysis.proficient: Systematically explores feature relationships using scatter plots and identifies strong predictors
dataAnalysis.advanced: Identifies complex relationships, detects outliers, and explains how feature engineering could improve predictions

modelBuilding.beginning: Cannot train a model or interpret MSE/R-squared values
modelBuilding.developing: Trains a model but cannot compare different feature sets or explain metric improvements
modelBuilding.proficient: Trains multiple models with different features, compares metrics, and selects the best configuration
modelBuilding.advanced: Systematically optimizes feature selection, identifies overfitting risks, and validates predictions against domain knowledge

criticalThinking.beginning: Accepts all model predictions without questioning their reasonableness
criticalThinking.developing: Notices some unreasonable predictions but cannot explain why they occur
criticalThinking.proficient: Identifies unreasonable predictions, connects them to data limitations, and suggests improvements
criticalThinking.advanced: Critically evaluates model assumptions, identifies bias in training data, and discusses ethical implications of automated pricing

strategies: Start with a single-feature model (square footage only) before adding complexity
Provide pre-made scatter plots and guide interpretation with labeled axes and trend lines
Use the ModelMentor guided tour to walk through each step of the regression workflow
Follow the standard lesson with the full multi-feature house price dataset
Encourage systematic experimentation with different feature combinations
Challenge students to identify and handle outliers in the dataset before training
Have students research non-linear regression and explain when linear models are insufficient
Ask students to create their own dataset for a different regression problem (e.g., predicting test scores from study hours)

handout.title: Regression Models: House Price Prediction - Student Handout
handout.instructions: Complete each section as you explore regression. Show your work and explain your reasoning.

sections.heading: Part 1: Exploring Relationships
prompts: Create scatter plots for at least 3 features vs. price. Which feature shows the strongest linear relationship?
Estimate the slope: for every additional 100 sq ft, how much does the price increase approximately?
reflectionQuestions: Are all relationships between features and price linear? Can you find one that is not?

sections.heading: Part 2: Training and Comparing Models
prompts: Model 1 features: ___ → MSE: ___, R-squared: ___
Model 2 features: ___ → MSE: ___, R-squared: ___

sections.heading: Part 3: Testing and Reflection
prompts: Enter a custom house description and record the predicted price. Does it seem reasonable? Why or why not?
reflectionQuestions: What factors affect house prices that are NOT in our dataset? How might this limit our model?
If you were buying a house, would you trust this model prediction? What else would you consider?

workflowSteps: Open ModelMentor and select Regression project type
Load the house price dataset and explore feature relationships
Select features and train your first regression model
Compare MSE and R-squared across different feature combinations
Test your best model with custom inputs and evaluate predictions

misconceptions.misconception: A model with low MSE on training data will always predict well on new data
misconceptions.correction: Low training MSE can indicate overfitting — the model memorized the training data rather than learning general patterns. Always test on unseen data.

misconceptions.misconception: Correlation between a feature and the target means that feature causes the target
misconceptions.correction: Correlation does not imply causation. A feature may correlate with price without directly causing it (e.g., number of bathrooms correlates with price because larger houses have more bathrooms).

misconceptions.misconception: Adding more features always improves the model
misconceptions.correction: Irrelevant or noisy features can actually hurt model performance. Feature selection is about finding the most informative inputs, not using everything available.

classroomTips: Use local real estate examples to make the dataset relatable — students engage more when they recognize the context
Have students predict prices manually first, then compare with the model — this builds intuition for what the model is doing
Connect MSE to the concept of average error in everyday terms: the model is off by about  on average

warmUp: How did you decide where to draw your line? What made some lines better than others?
directInstruction: Why do we square the errors in MSE instead of just averaging them? What would happen with negative errors?
If a model has R-squared of 0.85, what does that tell us? Is that good enough for predicting house prices?
guidedPractice: Which feature has the strongest relationship with price? How can you tell from the scatter plot?
closure: Would you trust this model to set the price for your house? What additional information would you want?
